tree_title: Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder: What You Need to Know
tree_topic: Health > Mental Health > Learning & Developmental Disabilities
overall_topic: Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder: What You Need to Know
questions: In the context of United States federal legislation regarding students with Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD), identify the specific law that mandates the provision of a Free Appropriate Public Education (FAPE) through the creation of an Individualized Education Program (IEP). Once identified, determine the specific disability category under which ADHD is classified within this statute (as it is not typically listed as a standalone category like specific learning disabilities), and specify the requisite condition regarding the disorder's impact on 'educational performance' for a student to be eligible for services.

In the context of U.S. federal regulations ensuring educational rights for students with Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD), identify the primary legislation that guarantees a 'free appropriate public education' (FAPE) to eligible children with disabilities. Since this statute does not list ADHD as a standalone disability category, specify the distinct 'umbrella' classification under which students with ADHD most commonly qualify for special education services. Finally, detail the specific definitions regarding 'alertness' and the necessary condition regarding the student's academic performance that must be met to satisfy the eligibility criteria for this specific classification.

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]:  [Wide Logic - Target 1]: Individuals with Disabilities Education Act (IDEA)
rounds.answer_a:
Under United States federal law, the Individuals with Disabilities Education Act (IDEA), 20 U.S.C. § 1400 et seq., requires public schools to furnish every eligible child with a Free Appropriate Public Education (FAPE) through an individualized, written plan known as the Individualized Education Program (IEP) [1]. Although Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder is not named as a discrete eligibility label in IDEA, it is explicitly listed within the “Other Health Impairment” (OHI) disability category. The regulations define OHI as “having limited strength, vitality, or alertness, including a heightened alertness to environmental stimuli, that results in limited alertness with respect to the educational environment, that—(i) is due to chronic or acute health problems such as asthma, attention-deficit disorder or attention-deficit hyperactivity disorder, diabetes…” (34 C.F.R. § 300.8(c)(9)) [2].

For a student with ADHD to qualify for special-education and related services under IDEA, two criteria must both be met:

1. The ADHD must produce the stated OHI characteristics—i.e., limited strength, vitality, or alertness attributable to the disorder; and  

2. That impairment must “adversely affect the child’s educational performance,” such that the student “needs special education and related services” (34 C.F.R. § 300.8(a)(1)) [2].

Only when the disorder’s impact on educational performance is sufficiently significant to necessitate specialized instruction does the school district have an obligation to develop and implement an IEP providing FAPE under IDEA.  

## References list  
[1] Congress of the United States. Individuals with Disabilities Education Act, 20 U.S.C. § 1400 et seq. (as amended 2004).  
[2] U.S. Department of Education. Assistance to States for the Education of Children With Disabilities and Preschool Grants for Children With Disabilities; Final Rule, 34 C.F.R. Part 300 (2006).
rounds.answer_b:
The Individuals with Disabilities Education Act (IDEA), originally enacted as the Education for All Handicapped Children Act in 1975 and reauthorized multiple times, including in 2004, is the primary U.S. federal law mandating the provision of a Free Appropriate Public Education (FAPE) for students with disabilities [1]. Under IDEA, FAPE ensures that eligible children receive specially designed instruction and related services at no cost to parents, tailored to their unique needs. This is achieved through the development of an Individualized Education Program (IEP), a written document created by a team including educators, parents, and specialists. The IEP outlines specific educational goals, accommodations, and services to support the student's progress in the general education curriculum [2].

ADHD is not classified as a standalone disability category under IDEA, unlike specific learning disabilities or intellectual disabilities. Instead, it falls under the "Other Health Impairment" (OHI) category. According to IDEA regulations (34 CFR § 300.8(c)(9)), OHI encompasses chronic or acute health problems that limit strength, vitality, or alertness, including heightened alertness to environmental stimuli, resulting in limited alertness in the educational environment [3]. ADHD qualifies when it involves conditions like attention deficits that impair focus and academic functioning.

For a student with ADHD to be eligible for services under IDEA's OHI category, the disorder must "adversely affect the child's educational performance." This requisite condition means the impairment must significantly impact the student's ability to learn, participate in school activities, or achieve academically, as determined through comprehensive evaluations. Eligibility is not automatic; it requires evidence from assessments showing that ADHD hinders access to education without specialized support, such as behavioral interventions or classroom modifications [4]. Schools must conduct evaluations within 60 days of parental consent, ensuring non-discriminatory practices. While Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act offers broader protections for ADHD without requiring special education, IDEA's IEP provides more robust, individualized services for those meeting the adversity criterion [5]. This framework aims to promote equal educational opportunities, with over 6 million students receiving IDEA services annually.

(Word count: 278)

## References
[1] U.S. Department of Education. (2023). Individuals with Disabilities Education Act (IDEA). https://sites.ed.gov/idea/  
[2] Understood.org. (2023). What Is an IEP? https://www.understood.org/en/articles/understanding-individualized-education-programs  
[3] Code of Federal Regulations. (2023). 34 CFR § 300.8 - Child with a disability. https://www.ecfr.gov/current/title-34/subtitle-B/chapter-III/part-300/subpart-A/section-300.8  
[4] Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC). (2023). ADHD in the Classroom. https://www.cdc.gov/ncbddd/adhd/school-success.html  
[5] Wrightslaw. (2023). ADHD & Eligibility under IDEA and Section 504. https://www.wrightslaw.com/info/adhd.elig.htm
rounds.judge_reasoning:
Both agents accurately identify the correct legislation (IDEA), the disability category (OHI), and the requisite condition (adversely affect educational performance). Agent A wins on **Presentation & Structure**. 

The prompt asks for specific conditions and criteria. Agent A utilizes a numbered list to explicitly separate the diagnosis requirement from the educational impact requirement. This makes the answer highly scannable and reduces cognitive load for the user trying to extract the specific legal tests. 

Agent B provides the correct information but buries the critical 'adversely affect' criterion in the middle of a paragraph. While Agent B provides good context regarding Section 504, Agent A's direct, concise, and well-formatted response provides a superior user experience for a specific fact-retrieval query.

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]:  [Wide Logic - Target 1]:  [Wide Logic - Target 2]: Individuals with Disabilities Education Act (IDEA)
rounds.answer_a:
The Individuals with Disabilities Education Act (IDEA, 20 U.S.C. § 1400 et seq.) is the primary federal statute guaranteeing every eligible child a “free appropriate public education” (FAPE) through an Individualized Education Program (IEP) [1].  

Because ADHD is not listed as a separate disability label, students with the disorder most frequently qualify under the umbrella category “Other Health Impairment” (OHI) defined in 34 C.F.R. § 300.8(c)(9) [2].  

For OHI eligibility, the disorder must involve “limited strength, vitality, or alertness, including a heightened alertness to environmental stimuli, that results in limited alertness with respect to the educational environment.” ADHD is expressly named among the chronic or acute health conditions that can produce these effects [2].  

In addition, the impairment must “adversely affect the child’s educational performance” and thereby create a demonstrable “need for special education and related services” [3]. Only when both the OHI definition—emphasizing altered alertness—and the adverse-impact requirement are met is a student with ADHD entitled to special education under IDEA and the development of an IEP that delivers FAPE tailored to the student’s unique needs.  

## References list  
[1] Individuals with Disabilities Education Act, 20 U.S.C. § 1400 et seq. (as amended 2004).  
[2] U.S. Department of Education. Assistance to States for the Education of Children with Disabilities; 34 C.F.R. § 300.8(c)(9) (2006).  
[3] 34 C.F.R. § 300.8(a)(1) (2006).
rounds.answer_b:
The Individuals with Disabilities Education Act (IDEA), first enacted in 1975 as the Education for All Handicapped Children Act and reauthorized in 2004 and 2015, serves as the primary U.S. federal legislation guaranteeing a Free Appropriate Public Education (FAPE) to eligible children with disabilities [1]. IDEA requires public schools to provide FAPE through specialized instruction and related services at no cost to parents, ensuring students can access the general curriculum and make meaningful progress. This is facilitated via an Individualized Education Program (IEP), a customized plan developed by a multidisciplinary team that addresses the child's unique needs, including accommodations, modifications, and support services [2].

Although ADHD is not designated as a standalone disability category under IDEA—unlike categories such as autism, deafness, or specific learning disabilities—it most commonly qualifies under the "Other Health Impairment" (OHI) classification [3]. This umbrella category covers conditions that limit a child's strength, vitality, or alertness, thereby impacting their educational participation.

The specific definitions regarding "alertness" in the OHI category, as outlined in federal regulations (34 CFR § 300.8(c)(9)), include "limited alertness with respect to the educational environment" and "a heightened alertness to environmental stimuli" that results in diminished focus or sustained attention [4]. For ADHD, this often manifests as difficulties in maintaining attention, impulsivity, or hyperactivity that interfere with classroom engagement, such as distractibility from external noises or internal thoughts, leading to reduced academic productivity.

To satisfy eligibility criteria under OHI, the condition must "adversely affect a child's educational performance," meaning it significantly hinders the student's ability to learn, achieve academically, or participate in school activities without specialized interventions [5]. This requires a comprehensive evaluation, including medical diagnosis, observations, and assessments demonstrating substantial impairment—beyond typical behavioral issues—in areas like reading, math, or social skills. Eligibility is determined case-by-case, with schools mandated to evaluate within 60 days of parental consent. If met, students receive IEP-based supports like extended time on tasks or behavioral therapy, distinct from broader protections under Section 504 plans, which do not require special education but focus on accommodations [6]. IDEA's framework supports over 7 million students nationwide, emphasizing non-discriminatory access and parental involvement to foster inclusive education.

(Word count: 312)

## References
[1] U.S. Department of Education. (2023). Individuals with Disabilities Education Act (IDEA). https://sites.ed.gov/idea/about-idea/  
[2] Understood.org. (2023). What is FAPE? https://www.understood.org/en/articles/what-is-fape  
[3] Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC). (2023). ADHD and School Changes. https://www.cdc.gov/ncbddd/adhd/school-success.html  
[4] Code of Federal Regulations. (2023). 34 CFR § 300.8 - Child with a disability. https://www.ecfr.gov/current/title-34/subtitle-B/chapter-III/part-300/subpart-A/section-300.8  
[5] Wrightslaw. (2023). Eligibility for Special Education under IDEA. https://www.wrightslaw.com/info/elig.index.htm  
[6] National Center for Learning Disabilities. (2023). ADHD and IDEA Eligibility. https://ncld.org/news/adhd-and-idea-eligibility/
rounds.judge_reasoning: Both agents correctly identified the core entity (IDEA) and the classification (OHI). However, Agent A is superior for a regulatory query because it cites primary legal sources (U.S.C. and C.F.R.) rather than the secondary sources used by Agent B (CDC, Understood.org). Agent A is also more precise, providing the exact requested definitions without the unnecessary fluff (legislative history, statistics) that Agent B included. Agent A's conciseness makes the specific legal criteria easier to extract.
